content.text: Hypercare exit is the formal decision to move a newly launched feature from its elevated operational posture — dedicated war room, heightened staffing, accelerated escalation SLAs — back to the standard steady-state support model. The exit decision is not based on elapsed time. It is based on meeting a set of objective criteria that demonstrate the feature has stabilized in production.

Exiting too early leaves the team without the resources to respond to the incidents that tend to surface in weeks 2–4 of a launch. Exiting too late holds feature engineers on rotation indefinitely, burning out the team and delaying the next launch. A clear, pre-agreed exit scorecard removes subjectivity from the decision.
Hypercare exit is a gate, not a timer. The 30-day nominal duration is a ceiling, not a target — a feature that meets all criteria in week 2 can exit then.

All five gates must be green before a hypercare exit review meeting can be called. A single red gate blocks exit regardless of how the others score. A partial (amber) gate requires explicit sign-off from the on-call SRE lead and product owner.

attrs.alt: Hypercare exit criteria scorecard — five gates with pass/fail status

content.text: The p99 error rate must remain at or below the pre-launch baseline for seven consecutive calendar days, with no P0-level spike during that window. A single P0 resets the seven-day clock.
p95 and p99 latency must be within 10% of the pre-launch baseline for seven consecutive calendar days. The baseline is measured from the 14-day window immediately preceding the launch date.

Actionable alert volume must drop to 5 or fewer per week for the last two consecutive weeks. Actionable means the alert required human intervention — informational or auto-resolved alerts are excluded. Additionally, no alert class may fire more than once in that period without a documented fix or suppression rationale.
Count only alerts that required a human action (acknowledge, investigate, mitigate).
Exclude alerts that auto-resolved without human involvement.
Any repeated alert class without a tracked remediation blocks this gate.
Alert volume above threshold for even one of the two required weeks resets the two-week clock.

Every P0 and P1 alert class must have a verified runbook with at least one successful use (tested by a drill or a real incident) and SRE lead sign-off. Coverage is measured against the alert inventory, not the runbook inventory — unwritten alerts without runbooks are a gap.
Alert inventory complete (all P0/P1 alerts listed)
Each alert has a linked runbook
Each runbook has been exercised at least once
SRE on-call has read all runbooks

All P0 incidents and all P1 incidents that missed their SLA during the hypercare period must have completed post-mortems. A post-mortem is complete when: it has been published in the incident doc system, all action items have a named owner and due date, and the feature team lead has reviewed it.
Draft post-mortems do not satisfy this gate — they must be published.
Action items may be in-progress; they do not need to be closed before exit.
A post-mortem in review with a named reviewer and a review-by date counts as complete for this gate.
P2 incidents do not require a post-mortem unless they triggered a P0 or were repeated.

When a team believes all five gates are green, they call an exit review meeting with the following attendees: the SRE lead, the feature engineering lead, the product owner, and the support lead. The meeting reviews the scorecard, surfaces any disputes, and produces a written exit decision.
Gate review: walk each gate with data (15 min). Any gate that is amber requires explicit discussion before proceeding.
Open incidents review: confirm no active P1 or P0 is open (5 min).
Pending action items review: confirm all post-mortem actions have owners and dates (5 min).
Transition plan confirmation: confirm steady-state rotation, escalation paths, and support handoff are ready (10 min).
Exit decision: unanimous sign-off from SRE lead, feature lead, and product owner (5 min).

If any attendee withholds sign-off, they must name a specific, measurable condition that, once met, would allow them to sign off. Vague objections ('I'm not comfortable yet') block exit indefinitely — require specificity.

# hypercare_exit_record.py — structured exit decision
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class HypercareExitRecord:
    feature_name: str
    exit_date: date
    launch_date: date

    # Gate results
    gate_error_rate: bool
    gate_latency: bool
    gate_alert_volume: bool
    gate_runbook_coverage: bool
    gate_postmortems: bool

    # Sign-offs
    sre_lead: str
    feature_lead: str
    product_owner: str

    # Transition
    steady_state_oncall_schedule: str
    support_handoff_doc_url: str
    amber_gate_waivers: list = field(default_factory=list)
    notes: str = ""

    @property
    def all_gates_green(self) -> bool:
        return all([
            self.gate_error_rate,
            self.gate_latency,
            self.gate_alert_volume,
            self.gate_runbook_coverage,
            self.gate_postmortems,
        ])

    @property
    def hypercare_duration_days(self) -> int:
        return (self.exit_date - self.launch_date).days


# Example exit record
record = HypercareExitRecord(
    feature_name="Claude API v2",
    exit_date=date(2024, 5, 14),    # exited after 28 days
    launch_date=date(2024, 4, 16),
    gate_error_rate=True,
    gate_latency=True,              # amber → waiver
    gate_alert_volume=True,
    gate_runbook_coverage=True,
    gate_postmortems=True,
    sre_lead="alice@anthropic.com",
    feature_lead="bob@anthropic.com",
    product_owner="carol@anthropic.com",
    steady_state_oncall_schedule="sre-prod-rotation",
    support_handoff_doc_url="https://internal.docs/claude-api-v2-steady-state",
    amber_gate_waivers=[
        "Latency gate: p99 within 12% (target 10%) for 5/7 days. "
        "Amber waiver granted by alice@: trend is clearly converging; "
        "remaining gap is in a low-traffic time window only."
    ],
)

assert record.all_gates_green, "Cannot exit with failed gates"
print(f"Hypercare duration: {record.hypercare_duration_days} days")
# → Hypercare duration: 28 days
The transition plan defines what changes operationally when hypercare ends. It is prepared at least one week before the expected exit date so that the steady-state team has time to prepare.
Standard: 10 min ACK, 30 min war room
Removed; incidents tracked in SRE weekly
SRE on-call rotation updated: hypercare schedule disabled, standard schedule active.
PagerDuty escalation policy updated from claude-hypercare-p0 to claude-prod-p0.
Feature engineering team removed from primary pager; added to tier-2 escalation.
All runbooks updated with steady-state contact information.
Support team briefed on expected behavior, known limitations, and escalation path.
Monitoring dashboards updated to show steady-state SLO thresholds (not hypercare thresholds).
Exit record published in team incident documentation system.
Hypercare Slack channel archived; incidents route to standard #incidents channel.

A team may request early exit (before 30 days) if all five gates are green and no P0 or open P1 exists. The exit review meeting follows the same process. Early exit does not imply the feature is 'easy' — it means the team met the criteria ahead of schedule.

A team may request an extension beyond 30 days if one or more gates remain amber or red with a credible convergence plan. Extensions are granted in 1-week increments. A second extension requires VP approval and a root-cause explanation for why the first extension did not resolve the gate.
Call exit review; proceed to transition
One amber gate, all others green
Call exit review; file waiver for amber gate
Red gate still present at day 37
Extend 1 more week; escalate to eng director
Escalate to VP; treat as systemic gap requiring dedicated sprint
An extension is not a failure. Treating a gate miss as a sign of a struggling team discourages honest reporting and leads to premature exits followed by post-exit incidents.
Manual scorecard tracking is error-prone and time-consuming. The exit scorecard can be automated by querying the monitoring, alerting, and incident systems that already hold the relevant data.

Hypercare exit requires all five objective gates to be green: error rate stability, latency regression, on-call alert volume, runbook coverage, and post-mortem completeness — each measured over a defined window, not a point-in-time snapshot. The exit decision is made in a formal review meeting with sign-off from the SRE lead, feature lead, and product owner. At exit, escalation SLAs relax, feature engineers leave the primary pager rotation, and the steady-state SRE rotation takes full ownership. Extensions are granted in one-week increments with a documented convergence plan; they are operational decisions, not judgments about team performance.
Five gates: error rate, latency, alert volume, runbook coverage, post-mortems — all must be green to exit.
Seven consecutive days of stability required for error rate and latency gates — a single P0 resets the clock.
Exit review meeting with SRE lead + feature lead + product owner required before transition.
Transition removes feature engineers from primary pager and relaxes escalation SLAs to steady-state values.
Extensions are granted in 1-week increments; two extensions require VP approval.
Automate the scorecard: query monitoring and incident systems rather than maintaining a manual spreadsheet.
